question: Which errors does an Error Boundary NOT catch?

options.text: Errors in event handlers (onClick, onChange)
Errors in asynchronous code (setTimeout, fetch)
Errors in the Error Boundary itself
Errors in the render() method of child components
options.explanation: Errors in render() of child components are exactly what Error Boundaries catch

options.text: Errors in constructors of child components
options.explanation: Errors in child component constructors are caught by Error Boundaries

question: Which lifecycle method updates the Error Boundary state on error?

options.text: static getDerivedStateFromError
componentDidCatch
options.explanation: componentDidCatch is used for side effects (logging), not for updating state

options.text: componentDidUpdate
options.explanation: componentDidUpdate is called on regular updates, not related to error handling

options.text: shouldComponentUpdate
options.explanation: shouldComponentUpdate controls re-rendering, not error handling

question: Why is having a single Error Boundary for the entire app a bad practice?

options.text: An error in one component crashes the entire UI instead of just a local part
A single Error Boundary cannot catch multiple errors
options.explanation: The number of caught errors is not limited — the problem is granularity

options.text: React forbids a single Error Boundary for the app
options.explanation: React doesn't forbid this, but it's bad practice for UX

options.text: A single Error Boundary doesn't work with nested components
options.explanation: Error Boundaries catch errors at any nesting depth

question: How do you handle an error in an onClick handler if Error Boundary doesn't catch it?

options.text: Wrap the code in try/catch and save the error in useState
Use a special onError prop on the Error Boundary
options.explanation: No such prop exists — Error Boundaries only catch render errors

options.text: Add .catch() after onClick
options.explanation: onClick is a synchronous handler, .catch() is not directly applicable to it

options.text: Errors in event handlers cannot be handled
options.explanation: Errors in event handlers are handled with regular try/catch

question: Why does fallback UI need a recovery (retry) button?

options.text: Many errors are temporary and can be resolved by retrying, otherwise the user is stuck
React requires a recovery button in every fallback
options.explanation: React doesn't require a recovery button — it's a recommendation for good UX

options.text: Without a recovery button, Error Boundary doesn't work
options.explanation: Error Boundary works without a button, but the user can't take any action

options.text: The recovery button automatically fixes the error
options.explanation: The button resets the error state and recreates the component tree, but doesn't fix the root cause
